title: Operator overloading

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C++: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Operator overloading; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: C++ Core Guidelines; living guidelines accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Operator overloading
sources.claim: An overloaded operator should preserve the operator's conventional meaning, invariants, const correctness, and relationships such as equality consistency.

canonicalExplanation.summary: An overloaded operator should preserve the operator's conventional meaning, invariants, const correctness, and relationships such as equality consistency.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: operator== compares all value-significant fields and is const, so equal objects produce equal observable value state. Your goal is to implement and test comparison or arithmetic operators with unsurprising semantics. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Value, reference and const-reference parameters; pf data records; Copy and assignment semantics; Overloading and default arguments. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: An overloaded operator should preserve the operator's conventional meaning, invariants, const correctness, and relationships such as equality consistency. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Precedence and arity cannot change, some operators must be members, implicit conversions affect symmetry, and surprising side effects make APIs unsafe. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: Operator overloading permits inventing new precedence or making + modify its left operand unexpectedly.

includes: Define only conventional operators that preserve value meaning and symmetry.
Implement and test comparison or arithmetic operators with unsurprising semantics.
excludes: Precedence and arity cannot change, some operators must be members, implicit conversions affect symmetry, and surprising side effects make APIs unsafe.

outcomes: Implement and test comparison or arithmetic operators with unsurprising semantics.

examples.title: Operator overloading: defined case
examples.situation: operator== compares all value-significant fields and is const, so equal objects produce equal observable value state.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: operator== compares all value-significant fields and is const, so equal objects produce equal observable value state.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Overloading changes implementation, not grammar; preserve conventional operator semantics and keep invariant and equality rules explicit.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Overloading changes implementation, not grammar; preserve conventional operator semantics and keep invariant and equality rules explicit.

examples.title: Operator overloading: boundary case
examples.situation: operator+ returns a new normalized value while operator+= mutates and returns *this by reference for conventional chaining.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: operator+ returns a new normalized value while operator+= mutates and returns *this by reference for conventional chaining.
Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Precedence and arity cannot change, some operators must be members, implicit conversions affect symmetry, and surprising side effects make APIs unsafe.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: Operator overloading permits inventing new precedence or making + modify its left operand unexpectedly.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement and test comparison or arithmetic operators with unsurprising semantics.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Operator overloading permits inventing new precedence or making + modify its left operand unexpectedly.
misconceptions.correction: Overloading changes implementation, not grammar; preserve conventional operator semantics and keep invariant and equality rules explicit.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: Operator overloading permits inventing new precedence or making + modify its left operand unexpectedly.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model Operator overloading: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
Precedence and arity cannot change, some operators must be members, implicit conversions affect symmetry, and surprising side effects make APIs unsafe.
